text:
FAT LOSS NUTRITION GUIDELINES

CALORIC DEFICIT
- Target: 300-500 kcal deficit below maintenance
- Aggressive deficit (>500 kcal) risks muscle loss
- Track weight weekly: aim for 0.5-1kg loss per week
- If no weight loss after 2 weeks, reduce calories by 100-200 kcal
- Never go below 1200 kcal (women) or 1500 kcal (men)

PROTEIN REQUIREMENTS (CRITICAL FOR FAT LOSS)
- Target: 1.8-2.4g protein per kg bodyweight
- Higher protein preserves muscle during deficit
- For a 70kg individual: 126-168g protein per day
- Protein has high thermic effect (burns calories during digestion)
- Increases satiety and reduces hunger

MACRONUTRIENT DISTRIBUTION
- Protein: 30-40% of total calories (muscle preservation)
- Carbohydrates: 30-40% of total calories (energy for workouts)
- Fats: 20-30% of total calories (minimum for hormones)

SATIETY-FOCUSED FOODS
High Volume, Low Calorie:
- Vegetables (spinach, broccoli, cauliflower, cucumber)
- Salads with minimal dressing
- Clear soups (vegetable soup, dal soup)
- Fruits (watermelon, papaya, oranges)

High Protein, Low Fat:
- Chicken breast (skinless)
- Fish (white fish, tuna)
- Egg whites
- Low-fat paneer
- Greek yogurt (low-fat)
- Whey protein isolate

INDIAN LOW-CALORIE MEALS
Breakfast Options:
- Poha with vegetables (light oil): ~200 kcal
- Moong dal chilla: ~150 kcal
- Oats upma: ~180 kcal
- Egg white omelette with vegetables: ~120 kcal

Lunch/Dinner Options:
- Grilled chicken + salad + 1 roti: ~350 kcal
- Dal + sabzi + 1 roti: ~300 kcal
- Fish curry (light) + vegetables: ~280 kcal
- Paneer tikka + salad: ~250 kcal

Snacks:
- Roasted chana: ~120 kcal per 30g
- Cucumber/carrot sticks: ~20 kcal
- Apple: ~80 kcal
- Buttermilk (chaas): ~40 kcal

MEAL TIMING FOR FAT LOSS
- Eat protein with every meal (controls hunger)
- Front-load calories (bigger breakfast/lunch, lighter dinner)
- Stop eating 2-3 hours before bed
- Consider intermittent fasting (16:8) if it fits lifestyle

FOODS TO MINIMIZE
- Fried foods (samosa, pakora, bhujia)
- Refined carbs (white bread, maida products)
- Sugary drinks (soda, packaged juices)
- High-calorie Indian sweets (gulab jamun, jalebi)
- Excessive oil in cooking (use spray or measure)

METABOLIC RATE CONSIDERATIONS
- Don't crash diet (slows metabolism)
- Include 1-2 refeed days per week (eat at maintenance)
- Prioritize strength training to preserve muscle mass
- Muscle burns more calories at rest than fat
- Sleep 7-8 hours (poor sleep increases hunger hormones)

HYDRATION
- Drink 3-4 liters water daily
- Water before meals increases satiety
- Green tea or black coffee (no sugar) can boost metabolism slightly

HUNGER MANAGEMENT
- Eat high-fiber vegetables with meals
- Drink water when hungry (sometimes it's thirst)
- Chew food slowly (20+ minutes per meal)
- Use smaller plates (psychological portion control)
- Plan meals in advance to avoid impulsive eating

TRACKING TIPS
- Weigh yourself same time weekly (morning, after bathroom)
- Take progress photos every 2 weeks
- Measure waist circumference
- Don't obsess over daily weight fluctuations (water retention varies)

SUSTAINABILITY
- Allow 1 cheat meal per week (not cheat day)
- Focus on whole foods 80-90% of the time
- Build habits, not just follow a diet
- Fat loss is a marathon, not a sprint

REGIONAL VARIATIONS

South Indian:

North Indian:

- Aloo Gobi (100g): 80 kcal, 2g protein, 12g carbs, 3g fats

PORTION GUIDELINES
- 1 fist = ~1 cup = ~150g cooked rice/dal
- 1 palm (thickness + size) = ~100g protein source
- 1 thumb = ~1 tbsp oil/ghee
- 2 hands cupped = ~1 serving vegetables

MEAL CONSTRUCTION FORMULA
Balanced Indian Meal:
- 1 portion protein (chicken/paneer/dal)
- 1 portion carbs (rice/roti)
- 1-2 portions vegetables
- Minimal added fats (1-2 tsp oil)
- Total: ~400-600 kcal depending on portions

MUSCLE GAIN NUTRITION GUIDELINES

PROTEIN REQUIREMENTS
- Target: 1.6-2.2g protein per kg bodyweight daily
- For a 70kg individual: 112-154g protein per day
- For muscle building, aim for the higher end (2.0-2.2g/kg)
- Distribute protein evenly across 4-5 meals (25-40g per meal)
- Post-workout protein: 20-40g within 2 hours of training

CALORIC SURPLUS
- Maintain a moderate surplus of 300-500 kcal above maintenance
- Aggressive surplus (>500 kcal) leads to excessive fat gain
- Track weight weekly: aim for 0.25-0.5kg gain per week
- If no weight gain after 2 weeks, increase calories by 200 kcal

MACRONUTRIENT DISTRIBUTION
- Protein: 25-30% of total calories
- Carbohydrates: 45-55% of total calories (fuel for training)
- Fats: 20-30% of total calories (hormone production)

INDIAN HIGH-PROTEIN FOODS
Vegetarian Sources:
- Paneer: 18g protein per 100g
- Tofu: 8g protein per 100g
- Moong dal (cooked): 7g protein per 100g
- Chana (chickpeas): 9g protein per 100g
- Greek yogurt: 10g protein per 100g
- Whey protein: 24g protein per 30g scoop
- Eggs: 6g protein per egg

Non-Vegetarian Sources:
- Chicken breast: 31g protein per 100g
- Fish (salmon, tuna): 20-25g protein per 100g
- Eggs: 6g protein per egg
- Mutton (lean): 25g protein per 100g

MEAL TIMING FOR MUSCLE GAIN
Pre-Workout (1-2 hours before):
- Moderate carbs + moderate protein
- Example: 2 rotis + paneer sabzi OR banana + peanut butter

Post-Workout (within 2 hours):
- High protein + fast-digesting carbs
- Example: Whey shake + banana OR chicken + rice

Before Bed:
- Slow-digesting protein (casein or paneer)
- Prevents overnight muscle breakdown

SAMPLE MEAL STRUCTURE
Breakfast: High protein + complex carbs
- Oats with whey protein, banana, nuts
- OR Egg bhurji with 2 rotis

Mid-Morning Snack:
- Greek yogurt with fruits
- OR Protein shake

Lunch: Balanced macros
- Chicken/paneer curry + rice + dal + salad
- OR Rajma rice with curd

Evening Snack (Pre-Workout):
- Banana + peanut butter
- OR Chana chaat

Dinner: Moderate carbs, high protein
- Grilled fish/chicken + vegetables + 1-2 rotis
- OR Dal + paneer + sabzi + roti

HYDRATION
- Minimum 3-4 liters water daily
- More during training days
- Dehydration impairs muscle recovery

CONSISTENCY TIPS
- Meal prep on weekends for busy weekdays
- Keep protein sources readily available
- Track intake for first 2-4 weeks to learn portions
- Don't skip meals, especially post-workout

PRE-WORKOUT & POST-WORKOUT NUTRITION GUIDE

PRE-WORKOUT NUTRITION

Timing:
- Eat 1-2 hours before training
- Allows digestion, prevents stomach discomfort
- Provides sustained energy during workout

Macronutrient Goals:
- Moderate carbohydrates (30-50g)
- Moderate protein (15-25g)
- Low fat (keeps digestion fast)
- Total: 200-400 kcal

Why Carbs Pre-Workout:
- Glycogen is primary fuel for intense training
- Prevents muscle breakdown during workout
- Improves performance and endurance
- Delays fatigue

Indian Pre-Workout Meal Options:

1-2 Hours Before:
- 2 rotis + paneer sabzi (light oil)
- Poha with peanuts
- Oats with banana and honey
- Rice with dal (small portion)
- Idli (2-3) with sambar

30-60 Minutes Before (Quick Options):
- Banana + peanut butter (1 tbsp)
- Apple + handful of almonds
- Dates (3-4) + milk
- Banana + whey shake
- Toast with jam

Hydration:
- Drink 300-500ml water 30 mins before
- Sip water during workout
- Avoid overhydration (causes stomach discomfort)

What to Avoid Pre-Workout:
- Heavy, fatty foods (slow digestion)
- Large meals (causes sluggishness)
- High-fiber foods (may cause GI distress)
- Spicy foods (heartburn risk)

POST-WORKOUT NUTRITION

The Anabolic Window:
- 30-120 minutes after training
- Muscles are most receptive to nutrients
- Glycogen replenishment is fastest
- Muscle protein synthesis is elevated

Macronutrient Goals:
- High protein (25-40g)
- Fast-digesting carbs (40-80g)
- Low-moderate fat
- Total: 300-600 kcal

Why Protein Post-Workout:
- Repairs muscle damage from training
- Stimulates muscle protein synthesis
- Prevents muscle breakdown
- Optimal dose: 0.3-0.5g per kg bodyweight

Why Carbs Post-Workout:
- Replenishes depleted glycogen stores
- Insulin spike helps shuttle nutrients to muscles
- Reduces cortisol (stress hormone)
- Improves recovery

Indian Post-Workout Meal Options:

Immediate (Within 30 mins):
- Whey protein shake + banana
- Chocolate milk (250ml) + 2 eggs
- Fruit smoothie with whey protein
- Banana + peanut butter + milk

Within 1-2 Hours (Full Meal):
- Chicken breast (150g) + rice (1 cup) + vegetables
- Paneer bhurji (150g) + 2 rotis
- Fish curry + rice
- Egg bhurji (4 eggs) + 2 rotis + fruit
- Dal (1 cup) + rice + paneer (100g)

Vegetarian Post-Workout:
- Whey shake + banana + oats
- Paneer tikka + rice
- Soy chunks curry + roti
- Greek yogurt + granola + honey + berries
- Tofu scramble + toast

Non-Vegetarian Post-Workout:
- Grilled chicken + sweet potato
- Tuna sandwich (whole wheat)
- Salmon + rice + broccoli
- Boiled eggs (4) + banana + toast

WORKOUT DURATION CONSIDERATIONS

Short Workout (<45 mins):
- Pre-workout: Light snack (banana)
- Post-workout: Regular meal within 2 hours

Moderate Workout (45-75 mins):
- Pre-workout: Moderate meal 1-2 hours before
- Post-workout: Protein + carbs within 1 hour

Long/Intense Workout (>75 mins):
- Pre-workout: Substantial meal 2 hours before
- Intra-workout: Consider BCAA or carb drink
- Post-workout: Large protein + carb meal immediately

TRAINING TIME VARIATIONS

Morning Training (Fasted):
- Some prefer fasted cardio for fat loss
- For strength training, at least eat banana + coffee
- Post-workout becomes breakfast (prioritize protein)

Evening Training:
- Lunch should be substantial (4-5 hours before)
- Light pre-workout snack 1 hour before
- Post-workout dinner within 1 hour

SUPPLEMENT CONSIDERATIONS

Effective Pre-Workout Supplements:
- Caffeine (coffee/green tea): Improves focus, energy
- Creatine (5g): Improves strength, power
- Beta-alanine: Delays muscle fatigue

Effective Post-Workout Supplements:
- Whey protein: Fast-digesting, convenient
- Creatine (5g): Muscle recovery and growth
- Glutamine: May reduce muscle soreness

Not Essential But Helpful:
- BCAAs: Useful if training fasted
- Carb powder (dextrose/maltodextrin): Fast glycogen replenishment

HYDRATION STRATEGY

Pre-Workout:
- 300-500ml water 30 mins before
- Well-hydrated urine should be pale yellow

During Workout:
- Sip 150-250ml every 15-20 mins
- For workouts >60 mins, consider electrolyte drink

Post-Workout:
- Drink 500-750ml water
- Rehydrate fully within 2 hours
- Monitor urine color

COMMON MISTAKES

Pre-Workout Mistakes:
- Training on completely empty stomach (low energy)
- Eating too close to workout (stomach discomfort)
- Too much fat/fiber (slow digestion)
- Not hydrating properly

Post-Workout Mistakes:
- Skipping post-workout meal (missed recovery window)
- Only protein, no carbs (incomplete recovery)
- Waiting too long to eat (>2 hours)
- Choosing slow-digesting proteins (casein, paneer)

GOAL-SPECIFIC RECOMMENDATIONS

For Muscle Gain:
- Pre: Moderate carbs + protein
- Post: High protein (30-40g) + high carbs (60-80g)
- Don't skip post-workout meal
- Consider mass gainer if struggling to eat enough

For Fat Loss:
- Pre: Light snack (banana + coffee)
- Post: High protein (30-40g) + moderate carbs (30-50g)
- Still eat post-workout (preserves muscle)
- Avoid liquid calories unless necessary

For Endurance:
- Pre: Higher carbs (50-80g)
- During: Carb drink for sessions >90 mins
- Post: Carb-focused with moderate protein

PRACTICAL MEAL PREP TIPS

Batch Prep:
- Cook chicken/paneer in bulk on weekends
- Pre-portion rice/rotis
- Keep bananas, peanut butter readily available
- Prepare whey shakes in advance (dry powder in shaker)

On-the-Go Options:
- Protein bars (check sugar content)
- Roasted chana + banana
- Peanut butter sandwich
- Boiled eggs (pre-cooked)
- Whey shake (portable)

TIMING SUMMARY

Pre-Workout Timeline:
- 2 hours before: Full meal (carbs + protein)
- 1 hour before: Light meal/snack
- 30 mins before: Quick carbs (banana, dates)

Post-Workout Timeline:
- 0-30 mins: Liquid meal (shake) or quick snack
- 30-120 mins: Full solid meal
- Beyond 2 hours: Benefits diminish but still eat

SAMPLE DAY SCHEDULE

Morning Gym (7 AM):
- 6:00 AM: Banana + black coffee
- 7:00-8:00 AM: Workout
- 8:15 AM: Whey shake + banana
- 9:00 AM: Full breakfast (eggs + roti)

Evening Gym (6 PM):
- 4:30 PM: Banana + peanut butter
- 6:00-7:00 PM: Workout
- 7:15 PM: Whey shake
- 8:00 PM: Dinner (chicken + rice + vegetables)

COMPREHENSIVE PROTEIN SOURCES GUIDE

PROTEIN QUALITY METRICS

Biological Value (BV):
- Measures protein absorption and utilization
- Higher BV = better quality protein
- Whey protein: BV 104 (highest)
- Egg: BV 100
- Chicken/Fish: BV 79
- Paneer: BV 84
- Soy: BV 74
- Legumes: BV 50-70

Complete vs Incomplete Proteins:
- Complete: Contains all 9 essential amino acids (animal sources, soy, quinoa)
- Incomplete: Missing one or more amino acids (most plant sources)
- Solution: Combine incomplete proteins (rice + dal = complete)

VEGETARIAN PROTEIN SOURCES

Dairy-Based (High Quality):
Paneer (100g):
- Protein: 18g
- Calories: 265 kcal
- Cost: ₹40-60 per 100g
- Bioavailability: Excellent
- Note: High in fat, use low-fat version for cutting

Greek Yogurt (100g):
- Protein: 10g
- Calories: 59 kcal
- Cost: ₹15-25 per 100g
- Bioavailability: Excellent
- Note: Low-fat, high-protein option

Milk (250ml):
- Protein: 8g
- Calories: 150 kcal
- Cost: ₹12-15 per 250ml
- Note: Good for post-workout recovery

Whey Protein (30g scoop):
- Protein: 24g
- Calories: 120 kcal
- Cost: ₹30-50 per serving
- Bioavailability: Highest
- Note: Fast-digesting, ideal post-workout

Legume-Based:
Moong Dal (100g cooked):
- Protein: 7g
- Calories: 105 kcal
- Cost: ₹5-8 per 100g
- Note: Easy to digest

Chana/Chickpeas (100g cooked):
- Protein: 9g
- Calories: 164 kcal
- Cost: ₹6-10 per 100g
- Note: High in fiber

Rajma (100g cooked):
- Protein: 8.7g
- Calories: 127 kcal
- Cost: ₹8-12 per 100g

Soy-Based:
Tofu (100g):
- Protein: 8g
- Calories: 76 kcal
- Cost: ₹25-40 per 100g
- Bioavailability: Good
- Note: Complete protein

Soy Chunks (100g dry):
- Protein: 52g
- Calories: 345 kcal
- Cost: ₹15-25 per 100g
- Note: Very cost-effective

Nuts & Seeds:
Peanuts (30g):
- Protein: 7.5g
- Calories: 170 kcal
- Note: High in fats

Almonds (30g):
- Protein: 6g
- Calories: 170 kcal
- Note: Expensive but nutrient-dense

Peanut Butter (2 tbsp):
- Protein: 8g
- Calories: 190 kcal
- Note: Choose unsweetened

NON-VEGETARIAN PROTEIN SOURCES

Poultry:
Chicken Breast (100g cooked):
- Protein: 31g
- Calories: 165 kcal
- Cost: ₹20-30 per 100g
- Bioavailability: Excellent
- Note: Leanest meat option

Chicken Thigh (100g cooked):
- Protein: 26g
- Calories: 209 kcal
- Note: Higher fat than breast

Fish & Seafood:
Rohu Fish (100g):
- Protein: 16.6g
- Calories: 97 kcal
- Cost: ₹25-40 per 100g
- Note: Rich in omega-3

Salmon (100g):
- Protein: 25g
- Calories: 208 kcal
- Cost: ₹80-120 per 100g
- Note: High omega-3, expensive

Tuna (canned, 100g):
- Protein: 30g
- Calories: 116 kcal
- Cost: ₹40-60 per 100g
- Note: Convenient, shelf-stable

Eggs:
Whole Egg (1 large):
- Protein: 6g
- Calories: 70 kcal
- Cost: ₹6-8 per egg
- Bioavailability: Perfect (BV 100)
- Note: Most cost-effective protein

Egg Whites (1 large):
- Protein: 3.6g
- Calories: 17 kcal
- Note: Pure protein, zero fat

Red Meat:
Mutton (lean, 100g):
- Protein: 25g
- Calories: 294 kcal
- Cost: ₹50-80 per 100g
- Note: Higher in fat and calories

PROTEIN TIMING & DISTRIBUTION

Daily Distribution:
- Spread protein across 4-5 meals
- Each meal: 20-40g protein
- Maximizes muscle protein synthesis
- Better than consuming all protein in 1-2 meals

Optimal Timing:
Morning (Breakfast):
- 25-35g protein
- Example: 3 eggs + 1 cup milk

Pre-Workout:
- 15-25g protein
- Example: Banana + peanut butter

Post-Workout (Critical):
- 25-40g protein
- Fast-digesting preferred (whey, chicken)
- Example: Whey shake OR chicken breast

Before Bed:
- 20-30g slow-digesting protein
- Example: Paneer OR casein protein
- Prevents overnight muscle breakdown

BUDGET-FRIENDLY PROTEIN OPTIONS

Most Cost-Effective (₹ per 10g protein):
1. Eggs: ₹10-12
2. Moong dal: ₹7-10
3. Soy chunks: ₹3-5
4. Chana: ₹7-10
5. Chicken breast: ₹6-10
6. Milk: ₹15-20
7. Paneer: ₹22-33
8. Whey protein: ₹12-20

Budget Meal Plan (150g protein):
- Breakfast: 4 eggs (24g protein) - ₹32
- Lunch: Chicken breast 150g (46g protein) - ₹45
- Snack: Whey shake (24g protein) - ₹40
- Dinner: Dal 200g + Paneer 100g (32g + 18g = 50g protein) - ₹60
- Total: 144g protein for ~₹177

PROTEIN COMBINING (For Vegetarians)

Complete Protein Combinations:
- Rice + Dal (complementary amino acids)
- Roti + Chana
- Peanut butter + Whole wheat bread
- Hummus + Pita

PROTEIN ABSORPTION TIPS

Maximize Absorption:
- Don't exceed 40g protein per meal (excess not absorbed efficiently)
- Pair with vitamin C (improves iron absorption from plant proteins)
- Cook legumes properly (soaking + pressure cooking improves digestibility)
- Avoid excessive fiber with protein meals (can reduce absorption)

Digestibility:
- Whey protein: Fastest (absorbed in 30-60 mins)
- Chicken/Fish: Fast (2-3 hours)
- Paneer/Casein: Slow (4-6 hours)
- Legumes: Moderate (3-4 hours)

SPECIAL CONSIDERATIONS

For Muscle Gain:
- Prioritize high BV proteins (eggs, chicken, whey, paneer)
- Aim for 2.0-2.2g per kg bodyweight
- Include protein with every meal

For Fat Loss:
- Emphasize lean proteins (chicken breast, fish, egg whites, low-fat paneer)
- Protein has high thermic effect (burns calories during digestion)
- Increases satiety
- Aim for 1.8-2.4g per kg bodyweight

For Vegetarians:
- Combine incomplete proteins throughout the day
- Consider whey/casein supplementation
- Focus on paneer, dal, soy, Greek yogurt
- May need slightly higher total protein intake (2.0-2.5g/kg)
